input_candidates.truth_value: Development of regression models for the prediction of steel density from chemical composition, using thermodynamic analysis to account for cementite in ferritic steels and TiC in austenitic steels.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Collection of literature data on physical properties of steels and analysis of the influence of composition on density using regression analysis and computational thermodynamics, covering ferritic/low alloy steels and austenitic steels.

input_candidates.prediction_value: Literature data from 44 batches with varying compositions; investigated by regression analysis in two models assuming cementite presence.

input_candidates.truth_value: In ferritic steels, Cu and Mo increase density, while C (as cementite), Cr, Mn, S, Si, and V decrease density.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Literature data on physical properties of steels were collected from the open literature into a database. 44 batches of ferritic and low alloy steels and 64 batches of austenitic steels were investigated by regression analysis.

input_candidates.truth_value: In austenitic steels, TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, and Ti reduce density; Co, Cu, Mo, and Ni increase density.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Former studies reviewed include: Gale on P decreasing lattice parameter linearly; Hornbogen confirming P effect; Takeuchi et al. on Be and Si decreasing lattice parameter while Al, Ti, V, Ge, and Mo raise it; Zwell et al. on Ni increasing lattice parameter to about 15 atomic percent then reducing it, Mn decreasing density linearly, and Ti decreasing density; Leslie overview on dilute substitutional solid solutions in fcc iron; Richter covering 52 iron alloys focusing on temperature dependence and composition effects; and studies on oxides in sintered steels and cementite density from lattice parameters.

input_candidates.truth_value: For substitutional solutes where no regression coefficient is available, theoretical coefficients (1/ρ_i) can give reasonable density estimates.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Regression formulas from former studies: rho_x = sum(p_i * C_i^wt) or rho_x = rho_Fe + sum(q_i * C_i^wt) where q_i = p_i - rho_Fe. The present study uses 1/rho_x = sum(r_i * C_i^wt), where r_i is approximately 1/rho_i for substitutional solutes. Phase density mixture rule: rho_steel = sum(rho_j * F_j^vol).

input_candidates.truth_value: Significant deviations from ideal behavior are observed for Si and Mn in ferritic steels (covalent bonds in Si, anomalous Mn effect on Curie temperature), and for Si in austenitic steels.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table 1 general information for ferritic and low alloy steels: ranges of alpha-region and 2-phase region mass contents in percent, maximum concentrations, elemental densities rho_i in kg/m3, and apparent densities from literature for Al, C, Co, Cr, Cu, Mn, Mo, Nb, Ni, P, S, Si, Ti, V, W, and alpha-Fe. Notable apparent densities: C 2856-5872, Co 8271-8361, Cr 6873-7018, Cu 7974, Mn 7153-7375, Mo 10341-10954, Ni 7855-8170, P 4174-6262, S -8826, Si 2373-4212, Ti 5079-5168, V 6398-7651, W 12873, alpha-Fe 7874.

input_candidates.truth_value: The regression formula 1/ρ_x = Σ r_i C_i^wt(x) provided better correlation than the linear formulas used in earlier studies.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table 2 Model I regression for ferritic and low alloy steels (zero solubility in cementite): r_C = 1.380e-4 m3/kg (apparent density 7246 kg/m3, difference 24.6 percent); r_Cr = 1.384e-4 (7223, 1.2 percent); r_Cu = 8.477e-5 (11796, 32.2 percent); r_Mn = 1.524e-4 (6563, -12.1 percent); r_Mo = 1.076e-4 (9296, -9.6 percent); r_S = 4.046e-4 (2472, 26.1 percent); r_Si = 2.381e-4 (4200, 80.3 percent); r_V = 2.012e-4 (4970, -18.7 percent); r_alpha-Fe = 1.270e-4 (7876, 0.02 percent). Al, Nb, P, Ti insignificant. 2 sigma/r_i percentages provided.

input_candidates.truth_value: Maximum experimental error reported by Richter is ±0.01% for density measurements.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table 3 Model II regression for ferrite in ferritic and low alloy steels (cementite computed separately): r_Cr = 1.379e-4 (7250, 1.5 percent); r_Cu = 8.563e-5 (11678, 30.9 percent); r_Mn = 1.425e-4 (7019, -6.0 percent); r_Mo = 1.106e-4 (9042, -12.0 percent); r_Ni = 1.347e-4 (7423, -16.7 percent); r_S = 4.113e-4 (2431, 24.0 percent); r_Si = 2.326e-4 (4300, 84.5 percent); r_V = 2.403e-4 (4161, -31.9 percent); r_alpha-Fe = 1.270e-4 (7876, 0.02 percent). Al, Nb, P, Ti insignificant.

input_candidates.truth_value: The paper provides a collection of literature data on density of steels and reviews earlier studies on the effect of solutes on lattice parameter and density of bcc and fcc iron.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table 4 regression for austenitic steels: significant coefficients include r_TiC = 2.032e-4 (apparent density 4921, elemental 4910); r_C = 3.178e-4 (3146, elemental 2267); r_Co = 1.111e-4 (9001, 8900); r_Cr = 1.431e-4 (6986, 7140); r_Cu = 1.205e-4 (8298, 8920); r_Mn = 1.307e-4 (7648, 7470); r_Mo = 1.018e-4 (9823, 10280); r_N = 2.186e-4 (4575); r_Ni = 1.137e-4 (8798, 8908); r_Si = 2.436e-4 (4105, 2330); r_Ti = 1.890e-4 (5292, 4507); r_gamma-Fe = 1.231e-4 (8124). Al, Nb, P, S insignificant; V not determined. Minimum and maximum concentrations given for each element in mass percent.
